prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Arrr, gather 'round ye scallywags and I shall tell ye the great mystery of the heavens above!

Ye see, when the great blazin' sun sends its light across the vast ocean of the sky, that light be carryin' all manner of colors within it - like the many colors of a fine stolen jewel. Now, the sky itself be full of tiny... *tiny* particles, smaller than even the weevils in our hardtack, and when that sunlight comes crashin' into 'em, those particles be scatterin' the colors about like a cannonball through a merchant vessel's cargo hold!

But HERE be the clever bit, and I'll not have ye forget it - the blue light, she be scatterin' FAR more than the red and the yellow, on account of her shorter... her shorter waves, like the choppy seas near the cape versus the long rollin' swells of the deep Atlantic. So the blue light gets thrown in every direction across the whole sky, while the other colors sail on through mostly undisturbed, like a Navy frigate passin' a ship flyin' false colors.

So when ye look UP, ye see blue comin' at ye from every direction across the whole firmament!

And THAT, ye barnacle-covered bilge rats, is why the sky be blue. Now back to the riggin' with ye, before I have ye keelhauled!
